text: The patient is placed in a standard supine position.
Following the skin incision, the sternum is split with the sternal sole and the retractor positioned to reveal the anterior mediastinum.
Radical timectomy imposes the resection of the tumor included in all the mediastinic fat tissue up to the pericardium.
A partial infiltration of the upper right mediastinal pleura imposes its resection.
The thymic adipose tissue is dissected to reach the right brachiocephalic vein that is thus identified and isolated.
Right lower thymic pole is resected and the right mediastinal pleura opened to access into the right pleural cavity in order to isolate the arch of the azugos vein.
An endovascular stapler is used to cut the vein.
The superior vena cava is then carefully isolated above the atrial junction.
The right brachiocephalic vein is identified and isolated as well.
Inspection of the lesion confirmed the infiltration of both brachiocephalic veins to their junction and of the first trait of the superior vena cava.
Without any extracorporeal circulation support, all the vessels are cross clamped.
The tumor is resected with the incision of the vein's trunk, revealing an endovascular thrombus.
The lesion is then carefully removed, sparing the posterior side of the vessels but answering macroscopic tumor negative margins.
A single bovine pericardial patch is then shaped and used to reconstruct the superior cavals system.
A 6-0 continuous absorbable suture is performed to ensure the pericardial patch and conferring a tubular structure to the graft.
Sequential unclamping of the vessels is performed without signs of bleeding or stenosis.
Four thoracic drains are positioned in order to evacuate both pleural cavities and the mediastinum.
The medium sternotum is closed and the wound sutured with intradermic absorbable wires.
